content.text: FastAPI is a modern, high-performance Python web framework for building APIs with native async support, automatic OpenAPI documentation, and runtime request validation via Pydantic. It has become the framework of choice for agentic AI backends because it maps cleanly onto async LLM SDK calls, supports server-sent event streaming for token delivery, and auto-generates interactive documentation that doubles as a tool specification for LLM function-calling.

FastAPI is built on two libraries: Starlette (the ASGI web toolkit providing routing, middleware, WebSockets, and streaming) and Pydantic (the data validation library providing typed request/response models, JSON Schema generation, and coercion). FastAPI's core insight is that Python type annotations are a complete description of an API's schema — by annotating function parameters with Pydantic models, you simultaneously get runtime validation, serialisation, error responses, and the OpenAPI specification with no additional code.

A FastAPI application is an ASGI application. Every route handler is an async function (though sync functions are also supported and run in a thread pool automatically). When a request arrives, FastAPI extracts path parameters, query parameters, headers, and body fields from the request, validates them against the annotated types, calls the handler function, validates the return value against the declared response model, and serialises the response as JSON. If validation fails at any point, FastAPI returns a structured 422 Unprocessable Entity response listing every field that failed.

FastAPI was created by Sebastián Ramírez and first released in 2018. It has grown to be one of the most starred Python repositories on GitHub, used in production by companies like Uber, Netflix, Microsoft, and virtually every modern AI startup. It is the framework most commonly featured in Anthropic's agentic system tutorials precisely because it aligns with the async-first, streaming-capable, self-documenting properties that agentic APIs require.

attrs.alt: FastAPI request lifecycle with middleware, dependency injection, Pydantic validation, async event loop, and auto-generated docs

content.text: The agentic AI pattern — receive a request, call an LLM, optionally call tools, stream tokens back — maps almost perfectly onto FastAPI's feature set. LLM calls are I/O-bound and slow (seconds to tens of seconds), which is exactly the workload async Python was designed for. Streaming token responses are first-class in ASGI via
. Tool endpoints need clear JSON schemas that LLMs can call — FastAPI generates these from Pydantic models automatically.

The automatic OpenAPI documentation is a particularly underappreciated feature for AI development. When you build a tool server that an LLM agent will call, the agent needs a machine-readable description of what the endpoint accepts and returns. FastAPI's
endpoint provides exactly this. You can point an OpenAPI-to-tool-schema converter at it and get LLM-callable tool definitions with zero additional documentation effort.

) solves a problem that every production agentic system encounters: how to manage shared resources (LLM clients, database connections, API keys, configuration) across request handlers without global state. Dependencies are declared as typed function parameters, lazily instantiated, and automatically cleaned up after each request — including proper cleanup of database sessions and connection pools.
Pydantic validates every request body, path parameter, and query parameter. Invalid requests return structured 422 responses with field-level error messages before your handler runs.
are generated from your type annotations with zero additional work. The spec stays in sync with the code automatically.
and await LLM calls, DB queries, and HTTP tool calls concurrently without blocking other requests.
provides clean, testable injection of database sessions, auth checks, configuration, and shared clients.
Python type annotations enforce consistent types across request parsing, business logic, and response serialisation.
and async generators support server-sent events and chunked transfer for LLM token streaming.
async context manager initialises shared resources (connection pools, model clients) at startup and cleans them up at shutdown.
FastAPI is consistently ranked among the fastest Python frameworks in the TechEmpower benchmark — comparable to Node.js and Go for I/O-bound workloads.
the async model requires every library in the call stack to be async-compatible. Using a sync database driver inside an async handler blocks the event loop for all concurrent requests.
many tutorials and stack overflow answers reference Pydantic v1 (
). Check which version your project uses.
FastAPI apps with complex Pydantic models and many routes take longer to start than bare ASGI apps. Significant in serverless (Lambda) deployments.
for a single endpoint that does a quick synchronous lookup, Flask's simpler mental model may be more appropriate than FastAPI's dependency injection machinery.
FastAPI has no opinion on database access. You must choose and configure an async ORM (SQLAlchemy async, Tortoise ORM) or raw async driver (asyncpg) separately.
runs in the same process as the web server. For production agentic workloads, use Celery or ARQ for persistent task queues.
When FastAPI starts, it inspects every route function's type annotations, building a parameter model for each. It generates an OpenAPI specification from these models and serves it at
. Swagger UI and ReDoc are embedded HTML pages that load and render this spec interactively.

When a request arrives, FastAPI calls Starlette's routing layer, which matches the request path and method against registered routes. FastAPI then uses Pydantic to parse and validate the request — path parameters are extracted from the URL, query parameters from the query string, headers from the HTTP headers, and the body is JSON-parsed and validated against the declared Pydantic model. If any validation fails, FastAPI immediately returns a 422 response listing all violations.
After validation, FastAPI resolves dependencies declared with
in topological order. Each dependency function is called (or awaited, if async), and its return value is injected into the handler. Generators used as dependencies (with
) have their cleanup code run after the response is sent. The handler function is then called with the resolved values, and its return value is validated against the declared
A production FastAPI agentic service typically has the following layers:
creates the FastAPI app with lifespan, registers routers, and attaches middleware.
separate Python files containing related route groups (
with a prefix and tags, which is included in the main app with
reusable dependency functions for DB sessions, auth, config, and shared clients in
business logic (agent orchestration, tool dispatch) is in service classes injected via
cross-cutting concerns (CORS, request tracing, rate limiting) are attached as middleware layers above the routing layer.
You are building a new agent API from scratch. FastAPI should be the default choice for new Python agent APIs.
You need to stream LLM tokens to clients via server-sent events — FastAPI's
and async generators make this straightforward.
You want automatic OpenAPI documentation that can double as LLM tool specifications for function-calling agents.
Your agent calls external APIs, databases, or LLMs concurrently — FastAPI's async model handles all of these without thread overhead.
You need dependency injection for clean management of shared resources (LLM clients, DB connection pools, configuration).
You have a team familiar with Python type annotations — FastAPI turns this knowledge into API documentation and validation automatically.

FastAPI is less suitable when you are maintaining an existing Flask application (the migration cost may outweigh the benefits), when your workload is primarily synchronous and CPU-bound (neither WSGI nor ASGI helps here), or when you need a simpler tool with fewer concepts (Flask has a shallower learning curve for trivial endpoints).

# Install FastAPI and an ASGI server
pip install fastapi uvicorn[standard]

# For production with all common extras:
pip install fastapi uvicorn[standard] pydantic-settings httpx

# Run development server with auto-reload:
uvicorn main:app --reload --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8000

from fastapi import FastAPI
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field
from typing import Optional

app = FastAPI(
    title="Agent API",
    description="REST API for an LLM-powered agent service",
    version="1.0.0",
)

# Pydantic models define both request/response schema AND OpenAPI docs
class AgentQuery(BaseModel):
    query: str = Field(..., min_length=1, max_length=10000, description="User question")
    model: str = Field("claude-opus-4-5", description="Model identifier")
    max_tokens: int = Field(1024, ge=1, le=8096, description="Max tokens in response")
    temperature: Optional[float] = Field(None, ge=0.0, le=1.0)

class AgentResponse(BaseModel):
    id: str
    query: str
    response: str
    model: str
    input_tokens: int
    output_tokens: int

# Route decorator: method + path
@app.post(
    "/api/v1/agent/ask",
    response_model=AgentResponse,
    summary="Ask the agent a question",
    tags=["agent"],
)
async def ask_agent(body: AgentQuery):
    # FastAPI automatically: validates body against AgentQuery,
    # returns 422 on schema violation, serialises return value via AgentResponse
    import anthropic, uuid
    client = anthropic.AsyncAnthropic()
    message = await client.messages.create(
        model=body.model,
        max_tokens=body.max_tokens,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": body.query}]
    )
    return AgentResponse(
        id=str(uuid.uuid4()),
        query=body.query,
        response=message.content[0].text,
        model=body.model,
        input_tokens=message.usage.input_tokens,
        output_tokens=message.usage.output_tokens,
    )

# Path parameters: typed and validated automatically
@app.get("/api/v1/models/{model_id}", tags=["models"])
async def get_model_info(model_id: str):
    return {"id": model_id, "type": "language_model"}

from fastapi import FastAPI, Depends, HTTPException, Header
from typing import Annotated
import anthropic

app = FastAPI()

# ── Dependency: Anthropic async client (shared per request) ──────────────────
async def get_anthropic_client() -> anthropic.AsyncAnthropic:
    """Yields a shared Anthropic client. Could also initialise from config."""
    return anthropic.AsyncAnthropic()

# ── Dependency: API key authentication ───────────────────────────────────────
async def require_api_key(x_api_key: Annotated[str | None, Header()] = None):
    """Validates X-Api-Key header. Raises 401 if missing or invalid."""
    valid_keys = {"sk-test-123", "sk-prod-456"}  # in production, check DB/cache
    if x_api_key not in valid_keys:
        raise HTTPException(status_code=401, detail="Invalid or missing API key")
    return x_api_key

# ── Dependency: Database session ─────────────────────────────────────────────
from contextlib import asynccontextmanager

# Use a real async DB library (e.g. sqlalchemy async) in production
class FakeDB:
    async def save(self, data): return {"saved": True, **data}

async def get_db() -> FakeDB:
    db = FakeDB()
    try:
        yield db    # FastAPI handles generator cleanup automatically
    finally:
        pass        # db.close() here

# ── Route using multiple dependencies ────────────────────────────────────────
@app.post("/api/v1/completions")
async def create_completion(
    body: dict,
    api_key: Annotated[str, Depends(require_api_key)],     # auth
    client: Annotated[anthropic.AsyncAnthropic, Depends(get_anthropic_client)],  # LLM
    db: Annotated[FakeDB, Depends(get_db)],                 # DB
):
    message = await client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=512,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": body.get("prompt", "")}],
    )
    result = message.content[0].text
    saved = await db.save({"result": result, "key": api_key[:5]})
    return {"completion": result, "saved": saved}

from fastapi import FastAPI
from fastapi.responses import StreamingResponse
from pydantic import BaseModel
import anthropic
import json

app = FastAPI()
client = anthropic.AsyncAnthropic()

class StreamRequest(BaseModel):
    prompt: str
    model: str = "claude-opus-4-5"

@app.post("/api/v1/stream")
async def stream_completion(body: StreamRequest):
    """Streams LLM tokens as Server-Sent Events (SSE)."""
    async def generate():
        # Send SSE preamble comment to keep proxy connections alive
        yield "data: [START]\n\n"

        async with client.messages.stream(
            model=body.model,
            max_tokens=2048,
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": body.prompt}],
        ) as stream:
            async for text in stream.text_stream:
                # Escape newlines inside the data field
                payload = json.dumps({"token": text})
                yield f"data: {payload}\n\n"

            # Send final usage stats in the last event
            msg = await stream.get_final_message()
            usage = {
                "input_tokens": msg.usage.input_tokens,
                "output_tokens": msg.usage.output_tokens,
            }
            yield f"data: {json.dumps({'done': True, 'usage': usage})}\n\n"

    return StreamingResponse(
        generate(),
        media_type="text/event-stream",
        headers={
            "Cache-Control": "no-cache",
            "X-Accel-Buffering": "no",   # disable nginx output buffering
        },
    )

from contextlib import asynccontextmanager
from fastapi import FastAPI
import anthropic, httpx

# Shared resources — initialised once, shared across all requests
state: dict = {}

@asynccontextmanager
async def lifespan(app: FastAPI):
    # ── Startup ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    state["llm_client"] = anthropic.AsyncAnthropic()
    state["http_client"] = httpx.AsyncClient(
        timeout=30.0,
        limits=httpx.Limits(max_connections=100, max_keepalive_connections=20),
    )
    print("Startup: clients initialised")

    yield  # Application runs here

    # ── Shutdown ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    await state["http_client"].aclose()
    print("Shutdown: clients closed")

app = FastAPI(lifespan=lifespan)

# Access shared resources from request state via dependency
from fastapi import Request

def get_llm_client(request: Request) -> anthropic.AsyncAnthropic:
    return state["llm_client"]

def get_http_client(request: Request) -> httpx.AsyncClient:
    return state["http_client"]
Middleware for CORS, request tracing, and logging

from fastapi import FastAPI, Request
from fastapi.middleware.cors import CORSMiddleware
from starlette.middleware.base import BaseHTTPMiddleware
import time, uuid, logging

app = FastAPI()

# ── CORS middleware ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
app.add_middleware(
    CORSMiddleware,
    allow_origins=["https://yourdomain.com"],   # restrict in production
    allow_credentials=True,
    allow_methods=["GET", "POST", "PUT", "PATCH", "DELETE"],
    allow_headers=["*"],
)

# ── Request ID + timing middleware ───────────────────────────────────────────
class RequestTracingMiddleware(BaseHTTPMiddleware):
    async def dispatch(self, request: Request, call_next):
        request_id = str(uuid.uuid4())
        start = time.perf_counter()

        # Attach request_id to request state for use in handlers
        request.state.request_id = request_id

        response = await call_next(request)

        elapsed_ms = (time.perf_counter() - start) * 1000
        response.headers["X-Request-Id"] = request_id
        response.headers["X-Response-Time"] = f"{elapsed_ms:.2f}ms"

        logging.info(
            f"method={request.method} path={request.url.path} "
            f"status={response.status_code} ms={elapsed_ms:.0f} "
            f"request_id={request_id}"
        )
        return response

app.add_middleware(RequestTracingMiddleware)
Background tasks for async agent runs

Consider building an evaluation harness for an LLM agent — a service that runs a suite of test prompts against the model and scores the outputs. The harness receives a batch of prompts, runs them all in parallel via
, collects results, and returns a structured evaluation report. Under FastAPI this looks like a single
endpoint returning 202 with a job ID, a background task running the parallel LLM calls, and a
polling endpoint returning status and results.
This pattern eliminates the need for a separate task queue for most agentic evaluation workloads. The FastAPI process handles both the web requests and the background LLM calls, with

managing parallelism across all prompts. A batch of 100 prompts that would take 100 seconds sequentially completes in roughly the time of the slowest single prompt (typically 5-15 seconds) because all 100 LLM calls are issued concurrently.
calling synchronous library functions directly inside
route handlers freezes all concurrent requests. Identify sync calls (
, sync database drivers, sync HTTP clients) and use
. Mixing the two causes subtle bugs.

using a module-level dictionary as a database works only with a single worker. With multiple Uvicorn workers, each process has its own memory. Use Redis or a database for any state that must survive across requests.
, FastAPI cannot validate or document the response shape. Undocumented response shapes break OpenAPI specs and LLM tool schemas.
unhandled exceptions in route handlers return a bare 500 with a traceback. Register
to return clean JSON error responses.
Not using lifespan for shared clients:
per request skips connection pooling and adds latency. Initialise shared clients in the lifespan context.
run after the response is sent but still in the same process. If the server crashes or restarts, pending background tasks are lost. For durable work, use a persistent task queue.
on every route. This validates your response schema and generates accurate OpenAPI documentation.
Use the lifespan async context manager (not
, which is deprecated) to initialise and clean up shared resources.
rather than global variables. This makes routes testable and keeps resource management explicit.
). Include routers in the main app with prefixes and tags.
Test routes by injecting mock dependencies via
in Pydantic models to add validation constraints (
), description strings, and example values that appear in the Swagger UI.
). Tags control how Swagger UI groups endpoints.

FastAPI is among the fastest Python web frameworks because it uses Starlette's ASGI router (which is close to bare uvloop performance) and Pydantic v2's Rust-based validation core. For I/O-bound agentic workloads, a single Uvicorn worker can maintain thousands of concurrent in-flight LLM calls with response times determined by LLM latency rather than framework overhead.

For CPU-bound tasks (embedding generation, reranking, image processing), FastAPI and async Python provide no parallelism benefit because the GIL prevents true multi-core execution. Offload CPU work to a process pool via
or route such work to a separate service.

Horizontal scaling follows the same pattern as any ASGI service: run multiple single-worker Uvicorn containers behind a load balancer. FastAPI imposes no constraint on horizontal scaling as long as application state is externalised (Redis for session state, PostgreSQL for persistent state). Using
with a single container is an option but complicates shared state — prefer multiple single-worker containers.

to identify real bottlenecks before optimising. The most common bottleneck in agentic FastAPI services is database connection exhaustion, followed by Pydantic validation overhead on very large response payloads, followed by synchronous library calls blocking the event loop.
the standard ASGI server for FastAPI. Run in development with
; in production behind a supervisor or Kubernetes.
FastAPI's validation backbone. Version 2 (default since FastAPI 0.100) uses a Rust core for 5-50x faster validation than v1.
extension for loading configuration from environment variables and .env files into typed Pydantic models. Essential for managing secrets.
the most complete async ORM for FastAPI. Combine with asyncpg for PostgreSQL or aiosqlite for SQLite.
database migration tool that works with SQLAlchemy. Generates migration scripts from model diffs.
async HTTP client for making REST API calls from route handlers. Supports connection pooling, streaming, and retries.
pytest plugins for testing async FastAPI routes. Use with
from httpx for async test scenarios.
rate limiting library for FastAPI using Redis for counter storage.
adds standardised pagination (page/size or cursor-based) to list endpoints with one decorator.
Simple APIs, legacy systems, sync workloads
Minimal ASGI apps, building frameworks on top
Alternative to FastAPI with different DI model
configure TLS termination at nginx or a load balancer, not inside FastAPI itself. Add an HTTPS redirect middleware if clients send plain HTTP.
FastAPI does not limit request body size by default. Set limits at nginx (
) and validate explicit size constraints with Pydantic field validators.
Pydantic validates types and constraints but does not sanitise HTML or SQL. Use parameterised queries for database calls and validate content is safe before storing or reflecting it.
(FastAPI's standard error type) and the base
to prevent stack traces from leaking to clients.
inject authentication as a dependency rather than checking in each handler. This makes it impossible to accidentally forget auth on a new route.
use Pydantic Settings to load secrets from environment variables or secret managers, never hardcode them. Validate that required secrets are present at startup via the lifespan context.

Think of FastAPI as a type-annotation-driven API contract system. Your Python type hints are the source of truth for three things simultaneously: (1) what data the route accepts and what it returns (Python type safety), (2) how incoming data is validated and what error to return on failure (Pydantic runtime validation), and (3) what the API specification looks like for consumers (OpenAPI/Swagger). You write the types once and get all three for free.

The dependency injection system is like a recipe card for each request. The recipe (route function signature) lists ingredients (dependencies). Before cooking (calling the handler), FastAPI gathers all ingredients in the right order — shared clients, database sessions, auth tokens — provides them to the cook, and then cleans up after the meal is served (running generator dependency teardown). This keeps each handler small and focused: it receives exactly what it needs, does its work, and returns a result.
FastAPI is an ASGI framework — all route handlers should be
and await I/O-bound operations to avoid blocking the event loop.
Pydantic models are the core abstraction: they define request validation, response serialisation, and OpenAPI schema generation all at once.
Use the lifespan async context manager to initialise shared resources (LLM clients, DB pools) at startup and clean them up at shutdown.
keeps route handlers small and makes them testable by overriding dependencies in tests.
with an async generator to stream LLM token output as server-sent events.
Never call synchronous libraries directly inside
endpoints are generated automatically from your type annotations — keep your models accurate and they stay accurate.
For production, deploy with Uvicorn and manage shared state externally (Redis, PostgreSQL) to enable horizontal scaling across multiple workers.
